content.text: Azure has two independent role systems that are frequently confused: billing/account roles and Azure RBAC roles. Billing roles control who can see invoices, manage payment methods, create subscriptions, and view cost data. Azure RBAC roles control who can create, modify, and delete Azure resources. A person can hold one type of role without the other — an Account Administrator can manage billing but cannot deploy a virtual machine unless they also hold a Contributor or Owner RBAC role.

attrs.alt: Azure Administrator Roles: billing/account roles vs Azure RBAC roles — two independent systems

content.text: Subscription Owner (Azure RBAC) and Account Administrator (billing) are not the same. The subscription Owner can manage all resources in the subscription. The Account Administrator originally signed up for Azure and controls billing — they may have no RBAC role at all if they never assigned one to themselves.

The classic billing roles predate Azure RBAC and originate from the original Azure account portal. They apply to Pay-As-You-Go and classic EA subscriptions. Microsoft is migrating customers to the MCA billing model, but classic roles remain in many organisations.
Create/cancel subscriptions, change billing contact, view all invoices
Classic portal admin — largely superseded by RBAC Owner
Same as Service Admin, up to 200 per subscription

Service Administrator and Co-Administrator are legacy roles from the classic Azure portal. They have no effect in the Azure Resource Manager model and should be replaced with Azure RBAC Owner assignments. Microsoft recommends migrating away from these roles.

# Check who the Account Administrator is for a subscription
az account show --query "{Sub:name,AccountAdmin:subscriptionPolicies.locationPlacementId}" -o table

# List classic Co-Administrators (legacy)
az role assignment list \
  --include-classic-administrators true \
  --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --query "[?roleDefinitionName=='CoAdministrator' || roleDefinitionName=='ServiceAdministrator'].{Role:roleDefinitionName,User:principalName}" \
  -o table

# Remove a Co-Administrator (migrate to RBAC)
az role assignment delete \
  --role "Co-Administrator" \
  --assignee "legacyadmin@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
EA customers have a hierarchy of billing roles that map to the EA portal hierarchy: enrollment → department → account → subscription. Each level has its own set of admins.
View all costs, create departments/accounts, manage EA admins, access all subscriptions' cost data
View all costs and billing data — no modifications
View department costs, create EA accounts within the department
Create subscriptions under the account, view account costs

# List EA enrollment accounts your identity has access to
az account list --query "[?tenantId!=''].{Name:name,Id:id}" -o table

# Get the enrollment account scope for creating subscriptions
az billing enrollment-account list \
  --query "[].{Name:name,Id:id}" -o table

# Create a subscription under an EA enrollment account
az account create \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0017P \
  --display-name "dept-engineering-prod" \
  --enrollment-account-object-id "<enrollment-account-object-id>" \
  --billing-account-name "<billing-account-id>"

# List all subscriptions under an enrollment account
az billing subscription list \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --query "[].{Name:displayName,Id:subscriptionId,Status:subscriptionBillingStatus}" \
  -o table
MCA uses a three-level billing hierarchy: billing account → billing profile → invoice section. Each level has its own set of roles with distinct permissions.
Full control: manage billing profiles, payment methods, all costs
Manage billing profiles and invoice sections — no payment method management
View all billing accounts, profiles, and costs (read-only)
Manage payment method, download invoices, create invoice sections
Manage invoice sections and subscriptions — no payment changes
View billing profile costs and invoices (read-only)
Create Azure subscriptions, manage subscription transfers

# List MCA billing accounts
az billing account list --query "[?agreementType=='MicrosoftCustomerAgreement'].{Name:displayName,Id:name}" -o table

# List billing profiles under an MCA account
az billing profile list \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --query "[].{Name:displayName,Id:name,Currency:currency}" \
  -o table

# List invoice sections under a billing profile
az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --profile-name "<billing-profile-id>" \
  --query "[].{Name:displayName,Id:name}" \
  -o table

# Assign Billing Profile Reader to a finance team member
az billing role assignment create \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --profile-name "<billing-profile-id>" \
  --role-definition-name "Billing Profile Reader" \
  --user-email-address "finance@example.com"

# List billing role assignments at the billing account level
az billing role assignment list \
  --account-name "<billing-account-id>" \
  --query "[].{User:userEmailAddress,Role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  -o table
Azure RBAC Roles for Resource Management

Azure RBAC controls access to Azure resources using role assignments. Each assignment has three parts: a security principal (who), a role definition (what permissions), and a scope (where — subscription, resource group, or individual resource).
IAM administrators who should not touch resources

# Assign Owner to a new subscription administrator
az role assignment create \
  --role "Owner" \
  --assignee "newadmin@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"

# Assign Contributor to a team (via Azure AD group — preferred)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --assignee "<group-object-id>" \
  --assignee-principal-type "Group" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>/resourceGroups/rg-dev"

# Assign Reader at subscription scope for auditors
az role assignment create \
  --role "Reader" \
  --assignee "auditor@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"

# List all Owner assignments on a subscription
az role assignment list \
  --role "Owner" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>" \
  --query "[].{Principal:principalName,Type:principalType,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Show all role assignments for a specific user across the subscription
az role assignment list \
  --assignee "engineer@example.com" \
  --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --all \
  --query "[].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Check what actions a role definition allows
az role definition list --name "Contributor" \
  --query "[0].{Name:roleName,Actions:permissions[0].actions,NotActions:permissions[0].notActions}" \
  -o json

# Show all built-in roles with their IDs
az role definition list --query "[?roleType=='BuiltInRole'].{Name:roleName,Id:name}" -o table | head -30

# Check if a user can perform a specific action (e.g., create a VM)
az role assignment list \
  --assignee "engineer@example.com" \
  --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --all -o json | python3 -c "
import sys, json
assignments = json.load(sys.stdin)
roles = [a['roleDefinitionName'] for a in assignments]
print('Roles held:', roles)
print('Can create VMs:', any(r in roles for r in ['Owner','Contributor','Virtual Machine Contributor']))
"

The Account Administrator role is assigned to whoever created the billing account. It can be transferred to another user — for example when an employee who originally signed up for Azure leaves the organisation.

# Account Administrator transfer is handled in the Azure portal:
# portal.azure.com -> Subscriptions -> <subscription> -> Transfer billing ownership

# For MCA subscriptions, use the billing API to transfer
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
BILLING_ACCOUNT="<billing-account-id>"

# Get the current billing account owner
az billing role assignment list \
  --account-name "$BILLING_ACCOUNT" \
  --query "[?roleDefinitionName=='Billing Account Owner'].{User:userEmailAddress}" \
  -o table

# Initiate a billing ownership transfer (MCA only)
# This is portal-only for classic PAYG subscriptions
curl -s -X POST \
  "https://management.azure.com/billing/billingAccounts/${BILLING_ACCOUNT}/billingProfiles/<profile>/invoiceSections/<section>/initiateTransfer?api-version=2020-12-15-privatepreview" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"properties": {"recipientEmailId": "newowner@example.com"}}'
Principle of Least Privilege in Practice

Granting Owner at the subscription level to everyone who asks is a common anti-pattern. Use the principle of least privilege: grant the minimum role at the narrowest scope that still lets the person do their job.
Ops needs to read all configs
Platform team manages RBAC for others
Security audit needs to read all settings

# Audit: find all Owner assignments at subscription scope (should be minimal)
az role assignment list \
  --role "Owner" \
  --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --query "[?scope=='/subscriptions/<subscription-id>'].{Principal:principalName,Type:principalType}" \
  -o table

# Identify service principals with Owner (often over-privileged)
az role assignment list \
  --role "Owner" \
  --subscription "<subscription-id>" \
  --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal'].{SP:principalName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Downgrade a service principal from Owner to Contributor
SP_OID="<service-principal-object-id>"
az role assignment delete --role "Owner" --assignee "$SP_OID" --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
az role assignment create --role "Contributor" --assignee "$SP_OID" --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
echo "Downgraded SP from Owner to Contributor"

When none of the 100+ built-in roles exactly fit your requirements — for example, you want to allow a team to restart VMs but not create or delete them — you can create a custom role. Custom roles list exactly which actions are permitted and denied.

# Create a custom role: VM Operator (can start/stop/restart, not create/delete)
cat > /tmp/vm-operator-role.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Name": "VM Operator",
  "Description": "Can start, stop, and restart virtual machines but cannot create or delete them.",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/powerOff/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/instanceView/read",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read",
    "Microsoft.Authorization/*/read",
    "Microsoft.Insights/alertRules/*"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"
  ]
}
EOF

az role definition create --role-definition /tmp/vm-operator-role.json

# Verify the role was created
az role definition list --name "VM Operator" \
  --query "[0].{Name:roleName,Actions:permissions[0].actions}" -o json

# Assign the custom role
az role assignment create \
  --role "VM Operator" \
  --assignee "ops-team@example.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"
Custom Role for Read-Only Storage with List Keys

# A storage admin who can list access keys but not modify storage contents
cat > /tmp/storage-key-reader.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Name": "Storage Key Reader",
  "Description": "Can list storage account keys and view storage configuration, but cannot modify data.",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listkeys/action",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listServiceSas/action",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "DataActions": [],
  "NotDataActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": [
    "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"
  ]
}
EOF

az role definition create --role-definition /tmp/storage-key-reader.json

# Update an existing custom role (add a new action)
az role definition update --role-definition '{
  "Name": "Storage Key Reader",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/read",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listkeys/action",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/listServiceSas/action",
    "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/blobServices/read",
    "Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read"
  ],
  "AssignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"]
}'

Azure AD Privileged Identity Management (PIM) adds just-in-time (JIT) access to high-privilege roles. Instead of permanently holding Owner or Global Administrator, a user is eligible for the role and activates it for a limited window (e.g., 1–8 hours) with justification and optionally MFA. PIM also provides an audit trail of every activation.
Eligible assignments: the user can activate the role for a time-bound window when needed.
Active assignments: the user permanently holds the role (no activation required — use sparingly).
Activation requires: MFA (optional), justification text (optional), approval from a designated approver (optional).
PIM alerts: detects permanent Owner assignments, stale assignments, and roles without MFA.

# PIM is managed via the Azure portal (Entra ID -> Privileged Identity Management)
# or via Microsoft Graph API. The following shows the Graph API approach.

TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://graph.microsoft.com --query accessToken -o tsv)

# List eligible role assignments for the current user
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/roleManagement/directory/roleEligibilityScheduleInstances?\$filter=principalId eq '<your-user-object-id>'" \
  --headers "Authorization=Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --query "value[].{Role:roleDefinitionId,StartTime:startDateTime,EndTime:endDateTime}" \
  -o table

# List active PIM role assignments
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/roleManagement/directory/roleAssignmentScheduleInstances?\$filter=principalId eq '<your-user-object-id>'" \
  --headers "Authorization=Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --query "value[].{Role:roleDefinitionId,AssignmentType:assignmentType,EndTime:endDateTime}" \
  -o table

# Activate a PIM-eligible role via Graph API (self-activate)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --resource https://graph.microsoft.com --query accessToken -o tsv)

# First, get the eligibility schedule ID for the role you want to activate
ELIGIBLE_SCHEDULE_ID=$(az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/roleManagement/directory/roleEligibilityScheduleInstances?\$filter=principalId eq '<your-user-id>'" \
  --headers "Authorization=Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --query "value[?roleDefinitionId=='<owner-role-id>'].id" -o tsv)

# Activate the eligible role for 2 hours with justification
az rest --method POST \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/roleManagement/directory/roleAssignmentScheduleRequests" \
  --headers "Authorization=Bearer $TOKEN" \
  --body '{
    "action": "SelfActivate",
    "principalId": "<your-user-object-id>",
    "roleDefinitionId": "<owner-role-definition-id>",
    "directoryScopeId": "/",
    "justification": "Performing emergency resource cleanup after incident #12345",
    "scheduleInfo": {
      "startDateTime": null,
      "expiration": {
        "type": "AfterDuration",
        "duration": "PT2H"
      }
    }
  }'
Regularly auditing role assignments helps catch over-privileged identities, stale accounts, and misconfigurations before they become security incidents.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# rbac-audit.sh — generate an RBAC inventory report

SUBSCRIPTION_ID="<subscription-id>"

echo "=== All Role Assignments (sorted by role) ==="
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --all \
  --include-classic-administrators true \
  --query "sort_by([].{Role:roleDefinitionName,Principal:principalName,Type:principalType,Scope:scope}, &Role)" \
  -o table

echo ""
echo "=== Owner Assignments (should be minimal) ==="
az role assignment list \
  --role "Owner" \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --query "[].{Principal:principalName,Type:principalType,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

echo ""
echo "=== Service Principals with Contributor or Higher ==="
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --all \
  --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal' && (roleDefinitionName=='Owner' || roleDefinitionName=='Contributor')].{SP:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

echo ""
echo "=== Guest Users with RBAC Assignments ==="
az role assignment list \
  --subscription "$SUBSCRIPTION_ID" \
  --all \
  --query "[?contains(principalName,'#EXT#')].{User:principalName,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Cross-check: find users with RBAC access but no recent sign-in (potential stale accounts)
# Requires Microsoft Graph read access

# Get all unique principal names from RBAC assignments
PRINCIPALS=$(az role assignment list \
  --subscription "<sub-id>" --all \
  --query "[?principalType=='User'].principalName" -o tsv | sort -u)

# For each, check last sign-in from Graph
for UPN in $PRINCIPALS; do
  LAST_SIGNIN=$(az rest --method GET \
    --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/${UPN}?\$select=signInActivity" \
    --query "signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime" -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
  echo "$UPN  $LAST_SIGNIN"
done

Azure has two independent role systems: billing/account roles (Account Administrator, EA Enterprise Administrator, MCA Billing Profile Owner) that control invoices and subscription lifecycle, and Azure RBAC roles (Owner, Contributor, Reader, User Access Administrator) that control resource creation and modification. Holding one type does not grant the other. Classic roles (Service Administrator, Co-Administrator) are legacy constructs from the original Azure portal and should be replaced with Azure RBAC assignments. Following the principle of least privilege — granting Contributor at resource group scope rather than Owner at subscription scope — reduces the blast radius of compromised credentials and accidental deletions.
